tools.name: grep
tools.description:
Fast content search tool that works with any codebase size.

- Searches file contents using regular expressions
- Supports full regex syntax (e.g., "log.*Error", "function\s+\w+")
- Filter files by pattern with the include parameter (e.g., "*.py", "*.{ts,tsx}")
- Returns file paths and line numbers with at least one match sorted by modification time
- Use this tool when you need to find files containing specific patterns
- If you need to identify/count the number of matches within files, use the bash tool with "rg" (ripgrep) directly.
- When you are doing an open-ended search that may require multiple rounds of globbing and grepping, use the sessions_spawn tool with agent_id="explore" instead

Prefer using grep for search tasks when you know the exact symbols or strings to search for.
tools.parametersJson: {"type":"object","properties":{"pattern":{"type":"string","description":"The regular expression pattern to search for in file contents."},"path":{"type":"string","description":"File or directory to search in. Defaults to the workspace root."},"include":{"type":"string","description":"Glob pattern to filter files (e.g. \"*.py\", \"*.{ts,tsx}\"). Only search files matching this pattern."},"ignore_case":{"type":"boolean","description":"Case insensitive search (default false).","default":false},"context_lines":{"type":"integer","description":"Number of context lines to show before and after each match. Default 0.","default":0}},"required":["pattern"]}

tools.name: glob
tools.description:
Fast file pattern matching tool that works with any codebase size.

- Supports glob patterns like "**/*.js" or "src/**/*.ts"
- Returns matching file paths sorted by modification time
- Use this tool when you need to find files by name patterns
- When you are doing an open-ended search that may require multiple rounds of globbing and grepping, use the sessions_spawn tool with agent_id="explore" instead
- You have the capability to call multiple tools in a single response. It is always better to speculatively perform multiple searches as a batch that are potentially useful.

Examples:
  - '*.js' (becomes '**/*.js') — find all .js files
  - '**/node_modules/**' — find all node_modules directories
  - '**/test/**/test_*.ts' — find all test_*.ts files in any test directory
tools.parametersJson: {"type":"object","properties":{"pattern":{"type":"string","description":"The glob pattern to match files against. Patterns not starting with \"**/\" are automatically prepended."},"path":{"type":"string","description":"Absolute path to directory to search for files in. If not provided, defaults to the workspace root."}},"required":["pattern"]}

tools.name: list
tools.description:
Lists files and directories in a given path.
The path parameter must be an absolute path; omit it to list the current workspace directory.

Use this tool when you need to explore the structure of a directory, discover available files,
or understand the layout of a project. For targeted searches by filename pattern, prefer the
Glob tool; for searching file contents, prefer the Grep tool.

Behavior:
- Directories are indicated with a trailing `/` suffix.
- Dot-files and dot-directories (e.g. `.env`, `.vscode/`) are hidden by default; set `show_hidden` to true to include them.
- Common non-essential directories (node_modules, .git, __pycache__, dist, build, etc.) are excluded by default.
- Results are sorted alphabetically by default, or by modification time with `sort_by: "modified"`.
- Results are capped at 100 entries; a summary is appended if truncated.
tools.parametersJson: {"type":"object","properties":{"path":{"type":"string","description":"Absolute path of the directory to list (e.g. \"/Users/me/project/src\"). If omitted, defaults to the current workspace root directory."},"ignore":{"type":"array","items":{"type":"string"},"description":"Additional glob patterns to exclude from results, on top of the built-in defaults (e.g. [\"*.log\", \"tmp\", \"coverage\"])."},"show_hidden":{"type":"boolean","description":"When true, includes dot-files and dot-directories in the output. Defaults to false."},"sort_by":{"type":"string","enum":["name","modified"],"description":"Sort order: \"name\" (alphabetical, default) or \"modified\" (by modification time, newest first)."}},"required":[]}

tools.name: read
tools.description:
Reads a file from the local filesystem. You can access any file directly by using this tool.
Assume this tool is able to read all files on the machine. If the User provides a path to a file assume that path is valid.
It is okay to read a file that does not exist; an error will be returned.

Usage:
- The file_path parameter must be an absolute path, not a relative path.
- By default, it reads up to 500 lines starting from the beginning of the file.
- You can optionally specify a line offset and limit (especially handy for long files).
- Any lines longer than 2000 characters will be truncated.
- Results are returned using cat -n format, with line numbers starting at 1.
- If you read a file that exists but has empty contents you will receive "File is empty."
- Supports document formats: PDF, DOCX, DOC, XLSX, XLS, PPTX, ODT, ODS, ODP, RTF — text is automatically extracted.
- Binary files (images, executables, archives, media) will return an error.

Efficient reading strategies:
- **Parallel multi-segment reads**: You can call read() multiple times in a SINGLE response with different offsets to sample non-adjacent sections of the same file simultaneously. For example, read lines 1-500, 1000-1500, and 2000-2500 in one batch to quickly understand a large file's structure.
- **Speculative batch reads**: When multiple files might be relevant, read them all in parallel rather than sequentially.
- **Targeted follow-up**: After sampling a file's structure, use grep to find exact locations, then read only those specific ranges.
tools.parametersJson: {"type":"object","properties":{"file_path":{"type":"string","description":"The absolute path of the file to read."},"offset":{"type":"integer","description":"The 1-based line number to start reading from. Only provide if the file is too large to read at once."},"limit":{"type":"integer","description":"Number of lines to read (default 500). Increase up to 2000 for larger reads, or use smaller values with parallel calls for sampling."}},"required":["file_path"]}

tools.name: write
tools.description:
Writes a file to the local filesystem.

Usage:
- By default, this tool will overwrite the existing file if there is one at the provided path.
- Use `append: true` if you only want to add content to the end of an existing file (e.g., for logs or incremental updates).
- If this is an existing file and you are overwriting it, you MUST use the Read tool first to read the file's contents. This tool will fail if you did not read the file first.
- ALWAYS prefer editing existing files in the codebase using the `edit` tool. NEVER write new files unless explicitly required.
- For large files, `edit` is MUCH safer and more token-efficient than `write`.
- NEVER proactively create documentation files (*.md) or README files. Only create documentation files if explicitly requested by the user.
- Only use emojis if the user explicitly requests it. Avoid writing emojis to files unless asked.
- Creates parent directories automatically if they don't exist.
- Keep each `content` under ~8192 tokens (rough estimate: floor(character length / 4), same as typical context accounting).
- If the full payload would exceed that, split into multiple writes: write in sequence, merging with Read between calls so each `content` stays under the token budget.
tools.parametersJson: {"type":"object","properties":{"file_path":{"type":"string","description":"The absolute path of the file to write."},"content":{"type":"string","description":"The content to write to the file. Aim for at most ~8192 tokens per call (estimate: floor(length/4)); if larger, batch via multiple writes and merge with Read between calls."},"append":{"type":"boolean","description":"If true, appends content to the end of the file instead of overwriting.","default":false}},"required":["file_path","content"]}

tools.name: edit
tools.description:
Performs exact string replacements in files.

Usage:
- ALWAYS prefer editing existing files in the codebase using this tool instead of overwriting with `write`.
- This tool is much safer and more token-efficient than `write` for large files because it only modifies specific blocks.
- You must use the Read tool at least once before editing. This tool will error if you attempt an edit without reading the file.
- When editing text from Read tool output, ensure you preserve the exact indentation (tabs/spaces) as it appears AFTER the line number prefix.
  The line number prefix format is: spaces + line number + tab. Everything after that tab is the actual file content to match.
  Never include any part of the line number prefix in the old_string or new_string.
- Only use emojis if the user explicitly requests it. Avoid adding emojis to files unless asked.
- The edit will FAIL if old_string is not found in the file.
- The edit will FAIL if old_string is found multiple times in the file. Either provide a larger string with more surrounding context to make it unique, or use replace_all to change every instance.
- Use replace_all for replacing and renaming strings across the file. This parameter is useful if you want to rename a variable for instance.
- old_string and new_string must be different.
tools.parametersJson: {"type":"object","properties":{"file_path":{"type":"string","description":"The absolute path to the file to modify."},"old_string":{"type":"string","description":"The exact text to replace (must match file contents exactly)."},"new_string":{"type":"string","description":"The replacement text (must be different from old_string)."},"replace_all":{"type":"boolean","description":"Replace all occurrences of old_string (default false). If true, replaces every instance in the file.","default":false}},"required":["file_path","old_string","new_string"]}

tools.name: apply_patch
tools.description:
Use the apply_patch tool to edit files. Your patch language is a stripped-down, file-oriented diff format designed to be easy to parse and safe to apply.

Format:
```
*** Begin Patch
[ one or more file sections ]
*** End Patch
```

Each operation starts with one of three headers:
- *** Add File: <path> — create a new file. Every following line is a + line (the initial contents).
- *** Delete File: <path> — remove an existing file.
- *** Update File: <path> — patch an existing file in place (optionally with *** Move to: <new_path> for rename).

Example patch:
```
*** Begin Patch
*** Add File: hello.txt
+Hello world
*** Update File: src/app.py
@@ def greet():
-print("Hi")
+print("Hello, world!")
*** Delete File: obsolete.txt
*** End Patch
```

Important:
- You must include a header with your intended action (Add/Delete/Update).
- You must prefix new lines with "+" even when creating a new file.
- Context lines start with " " (space), removed lines with "-", added lines with "+".
tools.parametersJson: {"type":"object","properties":{"input":{"type":"string","description":"Patch content using the *** Begin Patch/End Patch format."}},"required":["input"]}

tools.name: bash
tools.description:
Executes a shell command with optional timeout, ensuring proper handling and security measures.

IMPORTANT: This tool is for terminal operations like git, npm, docker, etc.
DO NOT use it for file operations (reading, writing, editing, searching) — use the specialized tools instead.

Usage notes:
- The command argument is required.
- You can specify an optional timeout in milliseconds (default: no timeout limit).
- Use the workdir parameter to run a command in a specific directory. AVOID "cd dir && cmd" patterns.
- It is helpful to write a clear, concise description of what the command does in 5-10 words.
- Avoid using bash for: find (use Glob), grep (use Grep tool), cat/head/tail (use Read),
  sed/awk (use Edit), echo > (use Write).
- Chain sequential commands with "&&", or make multiple parallel tool calls for independent commands.

MCP tools: Use `accio-mcp-cli` to discover and invoke MCP tools (Twitter, Gmail, Notion, Square, Apify, etc.).
  accio-mcp-cli keyword <keyword>   — fuzzy search tools by name/description (preferred, use first)
  accio-mcp-cli search [keyword]    — browse toolkit structure or filter by toolkit/service (fallback)
  accio-mcp-cli call <tool> [--key val | --json '{...}']  — invoke a tool
  IMPORTANT: Always wrap --json value in SINGLE quotes to prevent shell escaping issues.
    Correct: accio-mcp-cli call tool_name --json '{"orderId": "123"}'
    WRONG:   accio-mcp-cli call tool_name --json "{\"orderId\": \"123\"}"
tools.parametersJson: {"type":"object","properties":{"command":{"type":"string","description":"The shell command to execute."},"timeout":{"type":"integer","description":"Timeout in milliseconds (default: no limit). Command is killed after this period if specified."},"workdir":{"type":"string","description":"Working directory for the command. Use this instead of \"cd dir && cmd\"."},"description":{"type":"string","description":"Clear, concise description of what this command does in 5-10 words."}},"required":["command"]}

tools.name: process
tools.description:
Manage running exec sessions: list, poll, log, write, kill, remove.

Actions:
- list: Show all running and recently finished sessions.
- poll: Check the current status and output tail of a session.
- log: Read the full output log of a session (with optional offset/limit).
- write: Send input (stdin) to a running session (for interactive processes).
- kill: Terminate a running session (kills process group).
- remove: Remove a finished session from the list.

Use poll to check on background processes. Use log with offset/limit
to read specific sections of large output.
tools.parametersJson: {"type":"object","properties":{"action":{"type":"string","description":"Process action to perform.","enum":["list","poll","log","write","kill","remove"]},"processId":{"type":"string","description":"Process handle ID (required for all actions except 'list')."},"offset":{"type":"integer","description":"Character offset for 'log' action (default 0)."},"limit":{"type":"integer","description":"Character limit for 'log' action."},"input":{"type":"string","description":"Input text to send to stdin (for 'write' action)."}},"required":["action"]}

tools.name: cron
tools.description:
Manage scheduled tasks (cron jobs). Jobs persist in .accio/cron/jobs.json; run logs in .accio/cron/runs/{jobId}.jsonl.

ACTIONS:
- status: Check cron scheduler status (no other params)
- list: List jobs (includeDisabled: true to include disabled; in a conversation lists jobs bound to that conversation)
- add: Create job (requires name, schedule, payload; or pass full job object; id auto-generated)
- update: Modify job (requires jobId or id, plus patch object or flat fields)
- remove: Delete job (requires jobId or id)
- run: Trigger job once immediately (requires jobId or id)
- runs: Get job run history (requires jobId or id; optional limit)

Use jobId as the canonical identifier; id is accepted for compatibility.

JOB SCHEMA (for add; may be passed as job object or flat params):
{
  "name": "string (required)",
  "schedule": { ... },   // Required: when to run (see SCHEDULE TYPES)
  "payload": { ... },    // Required: what to run (see PAYLOAD TYPES)
  "conversationId": "CID-xxx",  // Required; binds the job to a conversation
  "sessionTarget": "main",  // Optional, only "main" supported (run in bound conversation)
  "enabled": true | false,   // Optional, default true
  "description": "string",   // Optional
  "deleteAfterRun": true | false   // Optional; default true for one-shot (at)
}

SCHEDULE TYPES (schedule.kind); exactly one:
- "in": One-shot after a delay from now (RECOMMENDED for "N minutes later"). Stored as "at"; no unit confusion.
  { "kind": "in", "inMs": <number> }   // delay in milliseconds from creation time
  Example: 10 minutes later → { "kind": "in", "inMs": 600000 }   (10*60*1000)
  Example: 1 hour later → { "kind": "in", "inMs": 3600000 }
- "at": One-shot at absolute time. Time MUST be in the future.
  Prefer ISO-8601 "at" to avoid unit mistakes; if using atMs it must be milliseconds since Unix epoch.
  { "kind": "at", "at": "<ISO-8601 timestamp>" }   // WITHOUT Z suffix = local time; WITH Z suffix = UTC
  { "kind": "at", "atMs": <number> }   // milliseconds since 1970-01-01 00:00:00 UTC
  TIMEZONE RULE: When the user says a local clock time (e.g. "9 PM tonight"), do NOT add Z suffix — use bare ISO-8601 like "2026-03-01T21:00:00". Adding Z treats the time as UTC, which is wrong for non-UTC users (e.g. UTC+8 user gets 05:00 next day instead of 21:00).
  Example (local time, recommended): { "kind": "at", "at": "2026-03-01T22:00:00" }
  Example (explicit UTC): { "kind": "at", "at": "2026-03-01T14:00:00Z" }
- "every": Recurring interval.
  { "kind": "every", "everyMs": <number>, "anchorMs": <optional-number> }
  everyMs: interval in milliseconds (e.g. 3600000 = 1 hour). anchorMs: optional first run time in ms since epoch.
- "cron": Standard cron expression — 5-field or 6-field.
  { "kind": "cron", "expr": "<cron-expression>", "tz": "<optional-IANA-timezone>" }
  5-field format: minute hour day-of-month month day-of-week
  6-field format: second minute hour day-of-month month day-of-week  (first field = seconds 0-59)
  Example: { "kind": "cron", "expr": "*/10 * * * *", "tz": "Asia/Shanghai" }      // every 10 min (5-field)
  Example: { "kind": "cron", "expr": "0 9 * * 1-5", "tz": "Asia/Shanghai" }       // 09:00 Mon–Fri (5-field)
  Example: { "kind": "cron", "expr": "*/30 * * * * *", "tz": "Asia/Shanghai" }    // every 30 sec (6-field)
  Example: { "kind": "cron", "expr": "0 */5 * * * *", "tz": "Asia/Shanghai" }     // every 5 min at :00 sec (6-field)
  Note: minimum reliable interval is ~2 seconds (scheduler refire gap).

PAYLOAD TYPES (payload.kind); exactly one:
- "command": Run shell command.
  { "kind": "command", "command": "<shell command string>" }
- "tool": Call a registered tool (e.g. question for simple reminder).
  { "kind": "tool", "tool": "<tool name>", "args": { ... } }
  Example reminder: { "kind": "tool", "tool": "question", "args": { "message": "该睡觉了" } }
- "agent": Run agent with message (LLM reply).
  { "kind": "agent", "message": "<prompt>", "agent": "<optional>", "model": "<optional>", "timeoutSeconds": <optional> }

SESSION TARGET (sessionTarget; applies when payload.kind=agent):
- Only "main" is supported: run in the bound conversation; messages appear in that conversation. Requires conversationId (set when adding from chat or explicitly).

CONSTRAINTS:
- One-shot (schedule.kind=at): default deleteAfterRun=true (job removed after success). Set deleteAfterRun=false to only disable and keep in list (cron list --include-disabled). On error or skipped we only disable, never delete.
- For "at" schedule the run time must be in the future; use ISO-8601 "at" or ensure atMs is milliseconds since epoch and > now.
- For "in N minutes" or "N分钟后" always use schedule { "kind": "in", "inMs": N*60*1000 } (e.g. 10 minutes = 600000); do NOT use "at" with a computed timestamp to avoid minute/second/hour confusion.
- After add succeeds, the returned job will show state:{nextRunAtMs:...} for at/every types. For cron type, state.nextRunAtMs is populated by the scheduler within 30 seconds — state:{} on the immediate add response is NORMAL and does NOT mean the job is broken. Do not call update just because state is empty.
tools.parametersJson: {"type":"object","properties":{"action":{"type":"string","enum":["status","list","add","update","remove","run","runs"],"description":"Action: status (no other params) | list (optional includeDisabled) | add (name+schedule+payload or job) | update (jobId+patch) | remove (jobId) | run (jobId) | runs (jobId, optional limit)."},"jobId":{"type":"string","description":"Job ID. Required for update, remove, run, runs. Canonical; id accepted as alias."},"id":{"type":"string","description":"Alias for jobId (use when jobId not provided)."},"job":{"type":"object","description":"For add: full job object { name, schedule, payload, conversationId, enabled?, description?, deleteAfterRun? }. When present, overrides flat params."},"patch":{"type":"object","description":"For update: object with fields to change (name, schedule, payload, conversationId, enabled, description, deleteAfterRun)."},"includeDisabled":{"type":"boolean","description":"For list: when true, include disabled jobs; when false, list only enabled jobs."},"name":{"type":"string","description":"Job name. Required for add (unless inside job)."},"description":{"type":"string","description":"Optional job description."},"enabled":{"type":"boolean","description":"Whether job is enabled. Optional, default true."},"schedule":{"type":"object","description":"For add/update: when to run. For \"in N minutes\" use { kind:\"in\", inMs: N*60*1000 } (e.g. 10 min = 600000); we convert to \"at\" at add. at: { kind:\"at\", at:\"<ISO-8601>\" } or { kind:\"at\", atMs:<number> } (ms since epoch). every: { kind:\"every\", everyMs:<number>, anchorMs?:<number> }. cron: { kind:\"cron\", expr:\"<expr>\", tz?:\"<tz>\" }."},"payload":{"type":"object","description":"For add/update: what to run. Must have kind \"command\"|\"tool\"|\"agent\". command: { kind:\"command\", command:\"<shell>\" }. tool: { kind:\"tool\", tool:\"<name>\", args:{...} }. agent: { kind:\"agent\", message:\"<prompt>\", agent?, model?, timeoutSeconds? }. For reminders use tool \"question\" with args.message."},"workspacePath":{"type":"string","description":"For run: optional workspace path (agent payload only)."},"conversationId":{"type":"string","description":"For add/update/run: conversation ID. Auto-bound from context when adding; optional override for run."},"deleteAfterRun":{"type":"boolean","description":"If true, delete job after one successful run. Default true for schedule.kind=at; set false to only disable and keep in list. On error/skipped we never delete."},"limit":{"type":"number","description":"For runs: max run log entries to return."},"sessionTarget":{"type":"string","enum":["main"],"description":"For add (agent payload only): where to run agent. Only \"main\" is supported: run in bound conversation."}},"required":["action"]}

tools.name: question
tools.description:
Send a standalone message to the user that appears in chat. Use ONLY for (1) cron-triggered notifications, or (2) a separate follow-up question/suggestion after you have already given your main reply.

Do NOT use this tool for normal conversational replies. When the user says hello or asks a question, reply directly with your assistant message — do not call this tool. Only use it for:
- **Scheduled notifications**: Cron jobs with payload kind="tool", tool="question", args={"message":"..."} (e.g. "该喝水了") so the text is shown when the timer fires.
- **Follow-up**: After completing a task, to send an extra question or suggestion (e.g. "Need me to set a recurring reminder?").

Required: message (string) — the text to show.
tools.parametersJson: {"type":"object","properties":{"message":{"type":"string","description":"The message or notification text to show to the user in the chat."}},"required":["message"]}

tools.name: web_search
tools.description:
Search the web for real-time information or images about any topic.
Returns summarized information from search results and relevant URLs.

Two modes:
- Text search (default): Returns web page titles, links, and snippets.
- Image search (image=true): Searches for existing images matching a keyword.
  Suitable for finding real-world photos, product images, brand visuals, or reference materials.
  Does NOT generate new images — use image_generate for that.

Use this tool when you need up-to-date information that might not be
available or correct in your training data, or when you need to verify
current facts.

IMPORTANT: Be specific and include relevant keywords for better results.
For technical queries, include version numbers or dates if relevant.
tools.parametersJson: {"type":"object","properties":{"query":{"type":"string","description":"Search query string. Be specific and include relevant keywords."},"image":{"type":"boolean","description":"Set to true to search for images instead of text-based web results. Default: false.","default":false},"count":{"type":"integer","description":"Number of results to return (1-20, default 8). For image search default is 10.","minimum":1,"maximum":20,"default":8},"country":{"type":"string","description":"2-letter country code for region-specific results (e.g., 'de', 'us', 'cn'). Default: 'us'. Only used for text search."},"language":{"type":"string","description":"Interface language for results. Use 'en', 'zh-cn', 'zh-tw', etc. Default: 'en'. Only used for text search."}},"required":["query"]}

tools.name: web_fetch
tools.description:
Fetches content from one or multiple URLs and returns their contents in a readable format.

Usage notes:
- Supports up to 10 URLs per request and fetches them in smaller batches.
- URLs must be fully-formed valid URLs.
- HTTP URLs will be automatically upgraded to HTTPS.
- batch_size is optional (1-5, default 3).
- parallel_batches is optional (1-4, default 2).
- timeout_seconds is optional (5-180, default 30).
- retry_count is optional (0-4, default 2).
- Output format: plain text (style/layout is not preserved).
- Images are returned as URL list only (no inline image rendering).
tools.parametersJson: {"type":"object","properties":{"urls":{"type":"array","items":{"type":"string"},"description":"One or more HTTP/HTTPS URLs to fetch. Maximum 10 URLs per request.","minItems":1,"maxItems":10},"timeout_seconds":{"type":"integer","description":"Optional timeout per fetch batch in seconds (5-180, default 30).","minimum":5,"maximum":180,"default":30},"batch_size":{"type":"integer","description":"Optional number of URLs per upstream batch (1-5, default 3). Smaller batches reduce timeout risk for slow sites.","minimum":1,"maximum":5,"default":3},"parallel_batches":{"type":"integer","description":"Optional number of fetch batches to run in parallel (1-4, default 2).","minimum":1,"maximum":4,"default":2},"retry_count":{"type":"integer","description":"Optional retry count for timeout and 5xx errors (0-4, default 2).","minimum":0,"maximum":4,"default":2}},"required":["urls"]}

tools.name: get_weather
tools.description: Get weather for a city. Supports current, forecast (up to 16 days), and historical queries.
tools.parametersJson: {"type":"object","properties":{"city":{"type":"string","description":"City name in English, e.g.: Beijing, Shanghai, Tokyo, New York. MUST use English name."},"date":{"type":"string","description":"Optional date in YYYY-MM-DD format. Omit for current weather. Supports future (up to 16 days) and past dates."}},"required":["city"]}

tools.name: get_time
tools.description: Get current date, time, timezone, and Unix timestamp. When the user mentions time (scheduling, deadlines, "now", dates, etc.), call this tool to ground answers in the real current time. Any time-related content before the current USER_MESSAGE (including assistant replies and older tool outputs) may be severely stale — the conversation context could span days. The system prompt date is static; do not rely on earlier turns or prior get_time results — call get_time in this turn whenever time relevance matters, so the answer uses fresh clock data.
tools.parametersJson: {"type":"object","properties":{},"required":[]}

tools.name: get_location
tools.description: Get current IP-based location: IP, city, country, coordinates, timezone, and ISP.
tools.parametersJson: {"type":"object","properties":{},"required":[]}

tools.name: task_create
tools.description:
Persistent, cross-agent task management — tasks are saved to disk and shared across all agents in the same conversation.
Use task_create/task_get/task_update/task_list for long-lived plans that survive session restarts and need multi-agent coordination.
For quick, ephemeral single-session tracking, use todowrite/todoread instead.

## Task Fields

- **subject**: A brief, actionable title in imperative form (e.g., "Fix authentication bug in login flow")
- **description**: Detailed description of what needs to be done, including context and acceptance criteria
- **activeForm** (optional): Present continuous form shown in the spinner when the task is in_progress (e.g., "Fixing authentication bug"). If omitted, the spinner shows the subject instead.

All tasks are created with status `pending`.

## Tips

- Create tasks with clear, specific subjects that describe the outcome
- Include enough detail in the description for another agent to understand and complete the task
- After creating tasks, use task_update to set up dependencies (blocks/blockedBy) if needed
- Check task_list first to avoid creating duplicate tasks
tools.parametersJson: {"type":"object","properties":{"subject":{"type":"string","description":"Brief, actionable title in imperative form"},"description":{"type":"string","description":"Detailed description with context and acceptance criteria"},"activeForm":{"type":"string","description":"Present continuous form for spinner display (optional)"},"taskListId":{"type":"string","description":"Task list identifier. Defaults to current conversation."}},"required":["subject","description"]}

tools.name: task_get
tools.description: Get full details of a persistent task (subject, description, status, dependencies, owner). Tasks are shared across agents in the same conversation.
tools.parametersJson: {"type":"object","properties":{"taskId":{"type":"string","description":"The ID of the task to retrieve"},"taskListId":{"type":"string","description":"Task list identifier. Defaults to current conversation."}},"required":["taskId"]}

tools.name: task_update
tools.description:
Use this tool to update an existing task's status, dependencies, or details.

## Common Operations

- **Mark in progress**: Set status to "in_progress" when starting work on a task
- **Mark completed**: Set status to "completed" when the task is done
- **Mark failed**: Set status to "completed" with subject prefixed by "[FAILED]" and failure reason in description
- **Mark skipped**: Set status to "completed" with subject prefixed by "[SKIPPED]" and reason in description
- **Set dependencies**: Use addBlockedBy to mark which tasks must complete before this one can start
- **Set blocks**: Use addBlocks to indicate which other tasks this task blocks
- **Assign owner**: Set owner to assign the task to a specific agent

## Tips

- Always mark a task as in_progress before beginning work
- Mark completed only after verifying the work is done successfully
- For failed tasks: update both subject (add "[FAILED]" prefix) and description (explain what went wrong), then set status to "completed"
- For skipped/deprioritized tasks: update subject with "[SKIPPED]" prefix and set status to "completed"
- NEVER leave tasks in in_progress when you finish responding — the user sees a progress panel that looks broken with orphaned in-progress tasks
- Use addBlockedBy/addBlocks to set up dependency chains after creating tasks
- Dependencies are bidirectional: addBlocks on task A with task B automatically adds addBlockedBy on task B with task A
tools.parametersJson: {"type":"object","properties":{"taskId":{"type":"string","description":"The ID of the task to update"},"status":{"type":"string","enum":["pending","in_progress","completed"],"description":"Update task status"},"subject":{"type":"string","description":"Update task title"},"description":{"type":"string","description":"Update task description"},"activeForm":{"type":"string","description":"Update spinner text"},"owner":{"type":"string","description":"Assign task to an agent"},"addBlockedBy":{"type":"array","items":{"type":"string"},"description":"Task IDs to add as blockers"},"removeBlockedBy":{"type":"array","items":{"type":"string"},"description":"Task IDs to remove as blockers"},"addBlocks":{"type":"array","items":{"type":"string"},"description":"Task IDs that this task blocks"},"removeBlocks":{"type":"array","items":{"type":"string"},"description":"Task IDs to stop blocking"},"taskListId":{"type":"string","description":"Task list identifier. Defaults to current conversation."}},"required":["taskId"]}

tools.name: task_list
tools.description: List all persistent tasks and their current status for this conversation. Shows task IDs, subjects, statuses, dependencies, and owners. Shared across agents in the same conversation.
tools.parametersJson: {"type":"object","properties":{"taskListId":{"type":"string","description":"Task list identifier. Defaults to current conversation."}},"required":[]}

tools.name: image_generate
tools.description: Generate a new image from a text prompt. Creates visual content based on the description — suitable for product mockups, concept art, illustrations, and creative design. Returns a CDN URL of the generated image.
tools.parametersJson: {"type":"object","properties":{"prompt":{"type":"string","description":"A detailed description of the image to generate. Be specific about style, composition, colors, and subject matter."},"aspect_ratio":{"type":"string","enum":["1:1","2:3","3:2","3:4","4:3","4:5","5:4","9:16","16:9","21:9"],"description":"Aspect ratio of the generated image. Default is '1:1' (square)."},"task_type":{"type":"string","enum":["simple","complex"],"description":"Generation complexity for model routing.\n- 'simple': Single product or simple scene, lower cost.\n- 'complex': Sets, posters, models, 3D-style or high-detail scenes."}},"required":["prompt"]}

tools.name: image_edit
tools.description: Edit an existing image based on text instructions. Supports background removal, object replacement, color adjustment, style transfer, composition changes, and more. Accepts one or more reference images (up to 14) and returns a CDN URL of the edited image.
tools.parametersJson:
{"type":"object","properties":{"prompt":{"type":"string","description":"A precise instruction describing the desired modification. E.g. 'remove the background', 'change the dress color to red'."},"reference_images":{"type":"array","items":{"type":"string"},"description":"List of image URLs to edit or use as reference. At least one required, up to 14."},"aspect_ratio":{"type":"string","enum":["1:1","2:3","3:2","3:4","4:3","4:5","5:4","9:16","16:9","21:9"],"description":"Aspect ratio of the output image. Default is '1:1'."},"task_type":{"type":"string","enum":["white_background","hd_upscale","watermark_removal","simple_generation","complex_generation"],"description":"Edit task category for routing to the right pipeline (white background, upscale, overlay removal, etc.).
- 'white_background': Product on pure white background.
- 'hd_upscale': Resolution / sharpness enhancement.
- 'watermark_removal': Remove watermarks, website or branding text, contact details, QR codes, and similar overlays.
- 'simple_generation': Straightforward edits (default).

tools.name: see_image
tools.description: **YOU CAN read and view images from the user's local filesystem!** This tool reads local files, HTTP URLs, or data URIs. I will automatically upload local files to make them accessible. Images are passed directly into your context as multimodal content — you will SEE the actual images and can analyze them yourself. **Pass 1–16 images per call**; split larger batches into multiple calls.
tools.parametersJson: {"type":"object","properties":{"image_paths":{"type":"array","description":"Array of image sources (1–16 per call). **Local file paths are fully supported** - I can read files from the user's filesystem. Also supports HTTP URLs and data URIs.","items":{"type":"string"}}},"required":["image_paths"]}

tools.name: memory_search
tools.description:
Semantically search long-term memory (MEMORY.md) and daily notes (diary/*.md).
Returns top snippets with path + lines.

When to use:
- When the user's message semantically refers to past work, prior decisions, previous conversations, dates, people, preferences, or todos — search memory before answering.
- If low confidence after search, say you checked but found nothing conclusive — never fabricate from memory.
- Use memory_get after memory_search to pull only the needed lines and keep context small.
tools.parametersJson: {"type":"object","properties":{"query":{"type":"string","description":"Search query — describe what you want to recall."},"max_results":{"type":"integer","description":"Maximum number of results to return (default 6, max 20).","default":6,"minimum":1,"maximum":20},"min_score":{"type":"number","description":"Minimum relevance score threshold (0.0–1.0, default 0.35).","default":0.35}},"required":["query"]}

tools.name: memory_get
tools.description:
Read specific lines from a memory file (long-term memory or daily diary).

Usage:
- Use after memory_search to pull only the needed lines and keep context small.
- Provide the path returned by memory_search.
- Use from_line and lines to read a specific range.
tools.parametersJson: {"type":"object","properties":{"path":{"type":"string","description":"Relative path to the memory file (as returned by memory_search)."},"from_line":{"type":"integer","description":"Starting line number (1-based). Default 1.","default":1,"minimum":1},"lines":{"type":"integer","description":"Number of lines to read (default 20, max 200).","default":20,"minimum":1,"maximum":200}},"required":["path"]}

tools.name: sessions_spawn
tools.description:
Launch a new sub-agent to handle a well-scoped task. Prefer multiple spawns when sub-tasks are independent so they can run in parallel. Keep each task to a manageable length (ideally completable in roughly a dozen steps).

Available agent types:
- explore: Fast read-only code-search specialist. Tools: glob, grep, read, list, memory_search, memory_get. Cannot modify files or run commands.
- bash: Shell command execution specialist. Tools: bash, process, read, list, glob, grep. MCP via bash: accio-mcp-cli (keyword, search, call). Cannot write/edit files or browse the web.
- browser: Web browsing and content extraction specialist. Best for: accessing user's authenticated pages and dashboards (social media analytics, e-commerce admin, CRM, email), visual inspection, interactive browsing (click/type/scroll), and JS-rendered or login-gated content. Uses the user's Chrome with active login sessions. Tools: web_search, web_fetch, browser (navigate/screenshot/snapshot/scroll/console/act), bash, read, write. Cannot edit code or generate images.
- general: Autonomous task executor with broad tools (files, bash, web, browser, images, memory, tasks). MCP: accio-mcp-cli via bash.
- accio_work_guide: Accio Work product knowledge specialist. Answers questions about Accio Work features, usage, configuration, and troubleshooting from onboarding docs and ~/.accio/ workspace. Read-only: web_search, web_fetch, glob, grep, read, list.

Usage notes:
- Each spawn should be independent: the sub-agent has no other context and should not depend on another spawn's output. Give each task everything it needs (URLs, scope, output format) to complete on its own.
- Control task length: aim for tasks that can be completed in roughly 10–15 steps (tool calls / reasoning). If a task would require many more steps, split it into smaller spawns. Avoid unbounded or very long-running tasks.
- Prefer launching multiple sub-agents in parallel for independent sub-tasks rather than one spawn that does everything sequentially.
- Each agent runs in an independent session. The result is NOT visible to the user — you must present or summarise it.
- Use task_create / task_list / task_update to plan and track sub-tasks when you spawn multiple agents; this gives the user visibility and avoids duplicate or missing work.
- Optional **delivery_path**: a folder path (relative to workspace or absolute) for the sub-agent to save deliverable files. The sub-agent is instructed to write results there; you can read from this path (e.g. with read, glob, grep) to get richer output beyond the returned message.

Writing effective task descriptions (keep each spawn self-contained and short):
- One spawn = one self-contained task, completable in roughly a dozen steps. Include exact URL(s), what to extract (fields, format), and any completeness rule (e.g. "ALL products on this page"). The sub-agent must not need results from another spawn.
- For paginated or multi-section work, consider one spawn per section or page range so each has an independent scope (e.g. "pages 1–5 only"). Avoid one spawn for "all pages" or "entire site" unless the scope is small.
- For browser tasks, say when web_fetch should be preferred over screenshots.

After receiving results:
- Check whether the result covers the scope you requested. If incomplete, follow up (sessions_send or another spawn) instead of treating partial results as final.
- Merge results from multiple sub-agents before presenting them to the user.
tools.parametersJson: {"type":"object","properties":{"task":{"type":"string","description":"Self-contained task description, ideally completable in roughly 10–15 steps. The sub-agent has no other context; include target URL(s), scope, required fields, and expected output format. Avoid tasks that would require very long execution — split into multiple spawns instead."},"agent_id":{"type":"string","enum":["explore","bash","browser","general","accio_work_guide"],"description":"Target sub-agent type."},"label":{"type":"string","description":"Short (3-5 word) description shown as progress indicator."},"model":{"type":"string","description":"Optional model override (e.g., 'gpt-4o', 'claude-sonnet-4-20250514')."},"delivery_path":{"type":"string","description":"Optional folder path (relative to workspace or absolute) for sub-agent deliverable output. The sub-agent will be instructed to save/write results to this path; you can read from it (read, glob, grep) to get richer results beyond the returned message."},"required_skills":{"type":"array","items":{"type":"string"},"description":"Skill names to inject into the sub-agent's system prompt. Pass this when the task requires specific knowledge or workflows defined in a skill, and the task description alone cannot substitute for that skill's content. Do not pass skills the sub-agent won't need."}},"required":["task","agent_id"]}

tools.name: sessions_list
tools.description:
List sessions with optional filters and last messages.

Use to:
- Check on spawned sub-agents and their status.
- View active sessions across the workspace, including main agent and sub-agent sessions.
- Find a session to send messages to or review history.
tools.parametersJson: {"type":"object","properties":{"kinds":{"type":"array","items":{"type":"string"},"description":"Filter by session kind: 'main', 'subagent', 'group', 'cron', 'hook', 'other'.","default":["subagent"]},"limit":{"type":"integer","description":"Maximum number of sessions to return (default 50).","minimum":1,"default":50},"active_minutes":{"type":"integer","description":"Only show sessions active within the last N minutes.","minimum":1},"message_limit":{"type":"integer","description":"Include last N messages per session (0-20, default 0).","minimum":0,"maximum":20,"default":0}},"required":[]}

tools.name: sessions_history
tools.description:
Fetch message history for a session with windowed pagination.

Use to:
- Review what a sub-agent has done and its findings.
- Read conversation history before sending a message.
- Understand the context of a previous interaction.

By default reads from the beginning (up to 50 messages).
Use offset + limit to paginate through longer histories.
The response includes total_messages so you know the full length.
tools.parametersJson: {"type":"object","properties":{"session_key":{"type":"string","description":"Session key (ID) to fetch history for."},"offset":{"type":"integer","description":"1-based message index to start reading from (default 1, i.e. from the beginning).","minimum":1,"default":1},"limit":{"type":"integer","description":"Maximum messages to return (default 50, max 200).","minimum":1,"maximum":200,"default":50},"include_tools":{"type":"boolean","description":"Include tool call/result messages (default false).","default":false}},"required":["session_key"]}

tools.name: sessions_send
tools.description:
Send a message into another session.

Use for:
- Checking on a sub-agent by asking it a question.
- Sending follow-up instructions to a running sub-agent.
- Agent-to-agent communication and coordination.
- Set timeout_seconds > 0 to wait for a response (sync mode).
  Set timeout_seconds = 0 for fire-and-forget.
tools.parametersJson: {"type":"object","properties":{"session_key":{"type":"string","description":"Target session key (ID)."},"label":{"type":"string","description":"Target session label (alternative to session_key)."},"message":{"type":"string","description":"Message content to send."},"timeout_seconds":{"type":"integer","description":"Wait for response up to N seconds. 0 = fire-and-forget (default 30).","default":30,"minimum":0}},"required":["message"]}

tools.name: product_supplier_search
tools.description: Search products or suppliers exclusively on Alibaba.com (alibaba.com). Only use this tool for Alibaba.com searches. Supports multimodal search (text + images).
tools.parametersJson: {"type":"object","properties":{"intent_type":{"type":"string","enum":["product","supplier"],"description":"Search intent: 'product' for product search; 'supplier' for supplier search."},"query":{"type":"string","description":"Product search query. MUST be in English. Format: '[product_name], [attr1], [attr2]'. Example: 'red dress, women, summer, casual'."},"reference_image":{"type":"string","description":"Image URL for visual search (recommended when images exist in context)."}},"required":["intent_type","query"]}

tools.name: listen_gmail_reply
tools.description:
Register a Gmail reply listener. When a new email from the specified addresses is received, this agent session will be reactivated with the email content.

The listener persists locally. A background polling task checks for new emails every minute. When a matching email arrives, the message is delivered to this agent session.

Call this tool with a list of email addresses to monitor. If called again with the same session, the previous config is replaced.

The listener has an expiration time (default 72 hours). After expiration it is automatically removed. Use expires_in_hours to customize.

To stop listening early, use the unlisten_gmail tool.
tools.parametersJson: {"type":"object","properties":{"listen_gmail_list":{"type":"array","items":{"type":"string"},"description":"List of email addresses to monitor. When a new email from any of these addresses is received, this session will be notified."},"expires_in_hours":{"type":"number","description":"How many hours this listener stays active. Default 72 (3 days). After expiration the listener is automatically removed."}},"required":["listen_gmail_list"]}

tools.name: unlisten_gmail
tools.description:
Remove specific email addresses from Gmail reply listeners.

Pass unlisten_gmail_list with the email addresses to stop monitoring. The addresses are removed from ALL matching listener configs across all sessions. If a config's monitor list becomes empty after removal, the entire config is deleted.
tools.parametersJson: {"type":"object","properties":{"unlisten_gmail_list":{"type":"array","items":{"type":"string"},"description":"Email addresses to stop monitoring."}},"required":["unlisten_gmail_list"]}
